coredata.dc:description: Curcumin (CUR) is among the most natural and effective antitumor drugs for cancer treatment. These drugs have low solubility and short half-lives that reduce their effectiveness in drug release systems. Herein, a hydrogel nanocarrier containing chitosan (CS), alumina (γ-Al2O3), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was prepared by the water-in-oil-in-water (W/O/W) double nanoemulsion method. DLS revealed a nanocarrier size of 227 nm, with a zeta potential of −37.8 mV, which corroborates its stability. FE-SEM showed its quasi-spherical shape, FT-IR and XRD confirmed the presence of all the components in the nanocomposite and gave information about the intermolecular interactions between them and the crystalline nature of the nanocarrier, respectively. The drug loading (48 %) and entrapment efficiency (86 %) were higher than those reported previously for other CUR nanocarriers. The drug release profile revealed a controlled and stable release, and a pH-sensitive behavior, with faster CUR release in an acid environment. The breast cancer cell line was examined by cytotoxicity and cell apoptosis analyses. The results showed that the slow release over time and the programmed cell death were due to interactions between CUR and the nanocarrier. Considering the results obtained herein, CS/γAl2O3/CQDs/CUR can be considered as a promising new nanosystem for tumor treatment.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Curcumin (CUR) is among the most natural and effective antitumor drugs for cancer treatment. These drugs have low solubility and short half-lives that reduce their effectiveness in drug release systems. Herein, a hydrogel nanocarrier containing chitosan (CS), alumina (γ-Al2O3), and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was prepared by the water-in-oil-in-water (W/O/W) double nanoemulsion method. DLS revealed a nanocarrier size of 227 nm, with a zeta potential of −37.8 mV, which corroborates its stability. FE-SEM showed its quasi-spherical shape, FT-IR and XRD confirmed the presence of all the components in the nanocomposite and gave information about the intermolecular interactions between them and the crystalline nature of the nanocarrier, respectively. The drug loading (48 %) and entrapment efficiency (86 %) were higher than those reported previously for other CUR nanocarriers. The drug release profile revealed a controlled and stable release, and a pH-sensitive behavior, with faster CUR release in an acid environment. The breast cancer cell line was examined by cytotoxicity and cell apoptosis analyses. The results showed that the slow release over time and the programmed cell death were due to interactions between CUR and the nanocarrier. Considering the results obtained herein, CS/γAl2O3/CQDs/CUR can be considered as a promising new nanosystem for tumor treatment.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer refers to many diseases characterized by the development of abnormal cells that divide uncontrollably and can to infiltrate and destroy normal body tissue. It is the second‑leading cause of death in the world. The invasion and metastasis stages take place as a result of an abnormal growth caused by an excessive multiplication of cancerous cells that attack the surrounding tissues [1]. Typically, cells from the primary site break away and spread to other tissues (metastasis) and cause problems with the process of feeding and oxygenating healthy cells, leading to the deterioration of healthy tissues [2]. Among cancer, breast cancer stands to be the leading cause of death for women all over the world, with over one million new cases diagnosed each year.

Chemotherapy is a popular way to treat cancer, but it has systemic toxicity and side effects, and only a small amount of medication reaches the cancer cells [3]. It may also develop multidrug resistance (MDR), which reduces therapy efficacy, protects cancer cells and induces DNA changes, causing cancer cells to develop and have a quick degradation. Expensiveness, limited targeting and uncontrolled drug delivery are other disadvantages of chemotherapy [4].

The development of drug delivery systems (DDS) has revolutionized cancer therapy. DDS can release drugs to specific organs or tissues, therefore improving medication tolerance, drug stability and half-life, as well as decreasing toxicity [5]. They can also be used to dispense single-dose drugs or to diminish the doses, as well as to preserve medications within the prescribed range or to transport them to particular locations [6]. However, conventional DDS are frequently poisonous, ineffective, and unstable. In addition, side effects, non-uniform sizes and low drug loading capacity are among the limitations of traditional DDS. To solve these issues, novel strategies are pursued.

One of the latest developments in cancer treatment is cancer nanotechnology. With the aid of this technology, pharmacist can prepare medications with higher therapeutic efficiency and fewer side effects. Nanotechnology has been developed to redesign new nanobiological DDS and to revolutionize drug delivery methods. Nanoscale DDS have a few side effects, good solubility, and pharmacological activity [7]. Micelles, liposomes, polymers, dendrimers, and nanogels are examples of organic compounds used as carriers in DDS.

Hydrogels are cross-linked polymeric 3D networks soluble in water, though can absorb large amounts of water and body fluids. Hydroxyl, carboxyl and amine moieties are examples of hydrophilic functional groups found in the main chains of hydrogels. Hydrogel networks can be built via physical cross-linking, chemical cross-linking, or both. Properties like mechanical strength and intracellular and extracellular transport are influenced by the chemical makeup of the hydrogel, its morphology, and equilibrium swelling [8,9]. Hydrogels are particularly attractive for medical applications due to their high water absorption, flexible structure, biodegradability and low protein absorption. They are used not only for drug release but also in other areas such as swelling in water, pH solutions and electrolyte solutions [10]. They are also applied in other fields such as tissue engineering due to their biocompatibility and mechanical similarity to body tissues [11]. Furthermore, many hydrogels display antibacterial properties, and can be used in wound healing applications [12]. However, certain limitations can reduce their use in drug release such as poor mechanical properties, bacterial resistance, and a small therapeutic window. To improve these weaknesses, inorganic nanoparticles can be added. Due to their high surface area, proper shape and pore diameter, they are suitable candidates as a part of hydrogels. These nanoparticles increase the drug efficiency and simultaneously improve the hydrogel mechanical strength, thus making them more biocompatible. In addition, the presence of inorganic nanofillers can improve the pH-responsivity of hydrogels [13–15].

Chitosan (CS), a natural carbohydrate polymer, has long been investigated for drug delivery and medical applications due to its biodegradability, biocompatibility and low toxicity. It has been frequently studied for the delivery of various drugs, vaccines, genes and chemotherapeutic agents, as well as for wound healing and antibacterial applications, and cancer therpy[15–19].

Gamma-alumina (γ-Al2O3) is extensively used in catalysis and adsorption owed to its high surface area and high degree of porosity [20]. Further, it shows many properties that make it suitable in DDS such as inexpensiveness, chemical and thermal stability, very low toxicity and good biocompatibility [21]. Its high porosity allows a high drug loading capacity, and its amphoteric nature and phase stability in water make it suitable for pharmaceutical applications. It is a good choice for use in drug delivery, cancer treatment, wound healing and immunotherapy [20].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a new class of carbon nanostructures with a particle size ≤10 nm. They were discovered in 2004 during the purification process of single-walled carbon nanotubes, and exhibit unique properties including biocompatibility, chemical inertness, water solubility, good light stability, low toxicity, cost effectiveness, and fluorescence activities similar to semiconductor quantum dots [22]. Their functional groups including NH, C-OH, and C-O-C can interact with functional groups of other biomolecules, which is interesting for the design of novel nanocomposites with improved properties [22].

Different anticancer drugs, both natural such as curcumin and quercetin, or synthetic like 5-Fluorouracil (5-FU) or cisplatin are currently used for cancer treatment [23]. Curcumin (CUR) (diferuloylmethane) is a natural polyphenol extracted from the rhizomes of the natural plant Curcuma longa L. (turmeric). Turmeric is one of the most broadly used culinary spices in India and Southeast Asian nations, and is widely used in traditional Chinese herbal medicine. Curcumin has numerous pharmacological activities including antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, antibacterial, antiviral, and anti-cancer activity [24], being the latter the most investigated over the last years. The main drawbacks facing the use of turmeric are its low water solubility and bioavailability [25].

Due to the low solubility of CUR, a nanocarrier should be used for its targeted release to cancer tissues [24]. In this study, a novel method to synthesize a pH-responsive drug delivery system based on a CS hydrogel was developed, with the aim to last longer in the blood cell and to have better release, distribution, and antitumor properties. The incorporation of γ-alumina and CQDs was very effective for improving the drug loading and entrapment efficiency. The hydrogel nanocomposite exhibited targeted and controlled release of CUR. To the best of our knowledge, DDS based on CS-γ-alumina-CQDs as nanocarriers have not been reported yet, hence this design is valuable and innovative. In addition, the double emulsion method was used to make a membrane around the nanocomposite that led to better drug retention. This approach results in spherical nanocarriers, with a smaller particle size. Span-80 surfactant was chosen as an emulsifier and bitter almond oil as a hydrophobic phase. The materials choice was performed taking into account numerous preliminary studies and the unique properties of each nanomaterial. Thus, bitter almond oil extracted by the green synthesis method was used as a hydrophobic dual emulsion to cover and trap the drug in the oil phase. The oil layer that separates the two water layers controls the drug release rate. The presence of multiple layers in the double emulsion system can increase the release time and develop a slow and sustained release profile for CUR, which is of paramount importance for drugs such as CUR that have a low bioavailability.

In this research, FTIR analysis was used to get insight about the interactions between the nanocarrier components. Additionally, XRD and FESEM were used to assess the crystalline structure and morphology of the developed nanocarrier, respectively. DLS and zeta potential provided information about its particle size, polydispersity and stability. The drug release kinetics was also investigated. Further, MTT and flow cytometry tests were performed to assess the anticancer potential of the synthesized CS/ γAl2O3/CQDs/CUR nanocomposite.

For the preparation of nanocarrier, Al (NO3)3.9H2O, ethanol, urea, acetic acid (99.5 %) and Span 80 surfactant were purchased from Merck Co. (Darmstadt, Germany). Chitosan (C12H24N2O9, medium molecular weight, DD: 81–96 %) and bitter almond oil in dialysis bags (12 kD cut off) were also purchased from Merck Co. Curcumin (1,7-bis(4-hydroxy-3-methoxyphenyl)-1,6-heptadiene-3,5-dione, C21H20O6, Mw = 368.38 g/mol), fetal bovine serum (FBS), Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM)/high glucose, trypsin/EDTA (0.25 %), penicillin/streptomycin, (3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide) (MTT) powder and annexin V-fluorescein isothiocyanate (FITC), were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (Schnelldorf, Germany). Double-distilled water was used to prepare all the solutions. All the reagents were of analytical grade and used without further purification.

ɣ-Alumina was synthesized via sol-gel process, following a method analogous to that described previously [20]. 35 g of Al (NO3)3.9H2O was added to 35 mL distilled water at 22 °C under magnetic stirring. Then, 72 g of urea was added to the solution, and the mixture was kept at 22 °C for 1 h before being filtered to remove any impurity. The saturated aluminum urea solution was heated at 90 °C for 12 h. The pH of the solution changed from 2 to 6 due to the formation of alumina during the reaction. The sol was heated for another 3 h to form a gel, which was heated in air at 280 °C for 1 h to remove any leftover urea or nitrate.

The CQDs were prepared by the hydrothermal method following a procedure similar to that reported by Liang et al. [26], with some modifications. In short, 0.5 g of chitosan was added to 100 mL of 1 % (v/v) acetic acid solution and dissolved by ultrasonication. The solution was filtered through a 0.45 μm pore size filter membrane to remove the insoluble material. Subsequently, the above mixture was poured into a stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 12 h. The resulting brown-black solution was filtered through a 0.22 μm pore size membrane and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove weight precipitate and agglomerated particles. A light brown aqueous solution of CQDs was obtained, that was kept at 4 °C in a refrigerator until further characterization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of chitosan/gamma-Al2O3/CQDs nanocarrier and curcumin drug loading
ce:section.ce:para: Firstly, 0.6 g of CS was added to 30 mL of 2 % (v/v) acetic acid and placed on a heater stirrer at room temperature to obtain a homogeneous 2 % (v/v) CS solution. Then, 0.3 g of gamma-Al2O3 powder was added to the obtained hydrogel until it was completely dissolved, and the solution was placed in an ultrasonic bath for 30 min. Then 25 mg of the synthesized CQDs was added to the above solution until it was completely homogenized on the heater stirrer. CUR was added to get a final drug concentration of 5 μg/mL, and was placed on a heating stirrer for 30 min to yield a CS/γ-alumina/CQDs/CUR nanocomposite. In the next step, 0.2 % (v/v) of Span 80 surfactant was added to the synthesized hydrogel. 4 mL of CUR-loaded-CS/γ-alumina/CQDs coated with Span 80 was injected dropwise with a syringe into 12 mL of bitter almond oil (hydrophobic phase) under vigorous stirring. After 15 min, 12 mL of distilled water was added dropwise to the hydrophilic phase; then, the microemulsion was removed from the stirrer and kept for 5 min to allow for layer separation. The oily phase was then separated with a sampler and centrifuged at 6000 rpm for 10 min to separate the nanocarrier from the aqueous phase. A freeze dryer was used to powder each sample in the different stages. For that purpose, samples were first placed in the refrigerator at −20 °C until they were completely frozen and then freeze-dried [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Drug loading and encapsulation effectiveness evaluation
ce:section.ce:para:
1 mL of PBS buffer and 1 mL of ethyl acetate were mixed with 1 mg of the nanocarrier (CS/γAl2O3/CQDs/CUR). The ethyl acetate phase was then separated from the mixture by stirring and the CUR content was measured by UV–Vis spectrophotometry at the maximum absorption wavelength of the free drug set at 419 nm. The percentage of CUR loading and encapsulation efficiency was calculated using Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively [28].

Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded on KBr pellets using a Thermo Nicolet Avatar 380 FTIR spectrophotometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA) at 25° in the wavenumber range of 600–4000 cm−1. Powder forms of CS, γAl2O3, CQDs, CS/γAl2O3, CS/γAl2O3/CQDs, and CS/γAl2O3/CQDs/CUR were analyzed by X-ray diffraction (XRD) at room temperature over the 2θ range of 10–80° using a Philips PW1730 diffractometer (Amsterdam, The Netherlands) equipped with a Cu Kα radiation source. The surface morphology of the nanocarrier was examined via Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) with a TESCAN, MIRA III microscope (Czech Republic). A particle size analyzer (Horiba, SZ-100, Japan) was used to carry out dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis and zeta potential measurements at 25 °C. The MTT assay and flow cytometry tests were used to characterize the nanocarrier cytotoxicity and cell apoptosis, respectively, using a Thermo Biomate 5 UV–Vis spectrophotometer (ThermoFisher Scientific, USA) working at 419 nm.

CUR release from the nanocarrier in PBS media at pH values of 5.4 and 7.4 (corresponding to the pH of cancerous and healthy tissues, respectively) was determined using the dialysis method [29]. 1 mL of the nanocarrier was submerged in 15 mL of PBS and placed in a dialysis bag at 37 °C (Mw cutoff 12 KDa). At predetermined time intervals of 0, 12, 24, 48, 72 and 96 h, 300 μL of the sample was taken and replaced with the same volume of fresh medium. The amount of drug released was estimated using a spectrophotometer set at 419 nm [27–29] following Eq. (3):
(3)
Curcumin Released
%
=
Curcumin Released
Curcumin Loaded
∗ 100
The percentage of cumulative drug release was calculated using a calibration curve.

To investigate the mechanism of CUR release from the nanocarrier at pH 7.4 and 5.4, experimental release data were fitted to different models, namely Zero order, First order, Higuchi, Korsmeyer-Peppas, Weibull, Baker Hopfenberg, Pepas-Salhin and Hixson-Crowell. The correlation coefficient (R2) or goodness of fit was used to determine the best-fitting model. Table 1 summarizes the equations used for modelling drug release kinetics [29–36].

The cytotoxicity of neat CUR, CS, CS/γAl2O3, CS/ γAl2O3/CQDs and CS/ γAl2O3/CQDs /CUR was evaluated on human breast cancer cell lines (MCF-7) using the MTT assay. Cells at a density of 8 × 104 cells/well were seeded in a 96-well plate and cultured for 24 h in a basic DMEM (Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % antibiotic penicillin/streptomycin at 37 °C in a 5 % CO2 humidified atmosphere. Then, they were treated with different concentrations of the above indicated samples (0.025, 0.05, 0.1, 0.15, and 0.2 g/mL). Similarly, control cells were cultured for 72 h in the laboratory. After 48 h, 20 μL of a 5 mg.ml−1 MTT solution in PBS (pH 7.4) was added to each well, and the plate was kept in the incubator at 37 °C for 4 h. Once the supernatant was discarded, 200 μL of DMSO solution was added to each well to dissolve the formazan crystals. The plate was shaken for 10 min, and the optical absorbance of the solution was measured at 570 nm using an ELISA reader. The cell viability and cell inhibition were calculated using Eqs. (4) and (5), respectively [37].

A V-FITC/PI apoptosis screening kit was used to analyze apoptosis using flow cytometry to identify and quantify the population of apoptotic and necrotic cells. Breast tumor cells (MCF-7 cells) were cultured with free curcumin, CS/γAl2O3/CQDs, CS/γAl2O3/CQDs/CUR and a control group for 24 h to assess the presence of hematite. After trypsinization, the modified cells were collected and centrifuged into 195 μL of annexin binding buffer V-FITC. Samples containing the cell suspension were then mixed with 5 μL of Annexin V-FITC solution and 10 μL of PI before incubation for 20 min at 37 °C in the dark. The cells were then examined by a flow cytometer [37].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size, shape, appearance and morphology evaluation

ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of a drug delivery nanocarrier is a key factor that conditions the drug release behavior. The appearance, surface morphology, nanoparticle size and potential presence of impurities of CS/γAl2O3/CQDs/CUR nanocomposite was investigated by FE-SEM, and typical images at different magnifications are shown in Fig. 1 . A uniform and smooth nanocarrier surface can be observed, without impurities, which corroborates the excellent compatibility between its components. The interfacial interactions between CUR CS/CQDs, and γAl2O3 prevent nanoparticle agglomeration, resulting in a homogenous system. The nanoparticles exhibit quasi-spherical shape, with size ranging from 25 to 47 nm. The spherical shape has been reported to be the most suitable for drug release applications [38]. Further, particle size affects the tumor microenvironment [39]. Nanocarriers smaller than 300 nm can penetrate the tumor vasculature through its leaky endothelium and accumulate at the tumor site. This is known as the enhanced permeation and retention (EPR) effect. Nanoparticles between 20 and 100 nm are internalized through caveolae-mediated endocytosis [39]. Therefore, CS/γAl2O3/CQDs is expected to penetrate deeply into tumors and improve the EPR effect.

The colloidal stability of the nanocomposite was examined via zeta potential measurements. Zeta potential can be obtained from the measured electrophoretic mobility via Smoluchowski's equation [42]. The mean mobility of the nanocarrier containing CUR anticancer drug was found to be −2.96 (μm/s) /(V/cm), and the mean zeta potential −37.85 mV (Fig. 3 ). This high value demonstrates the stability of the developed nanocarrier, since absolute values above −30 mV are indicative of good nanoparticle stability [16]. The highly repulsive electrostatic forces between nanoparticles prevent them from forming aggregates. The negative charge of CUR is a result of its keto-enol and hydroxyl groups, which are partly deprotonated in aqueous solutions. Recent studies have shown that nanocomposites and nanocarriers with negative charges are more biocompatible than those with positive charges [43,44]. Further, the negative charge will also contribute to its removal by macrophages and to a reduced cytotoxicity compared to positively charged. In addition, negatively charged nanocarriers move more slowly and remain in the bloodstream for a longer period, which makes them highly suitable for the design of DDS.

FTIR spectra (Fig. 4 ) were recorded to confirm the presence of all the components in the nanocarrier and to get information about the interactions among them. Besides, they corroborated the successful synthesis of gamma-alumina and CQDs.

The spectrum of neat CS (Fig. 4a) shows a wide peak centered at 3385 cm−1, associated to NH and OH stretching vibrations, and the shape indicates intermolecular hydrogen bonding. The band centered at 2937 cm−1 is attributed to CH stretching vibrations, in agreement with the results previously reported [13,15]. The peaks at 1589 cm−1 and 1390 cm−1 can be attributed to the CO and CN stretching of amide I and amide III bands, respectively. The NH bending of primary amines also appears at 1589 cm−1 [18], and the CO stretching can be observed at 1024 cm−1, The bands at 1420 and 1370 cm−1 are related to CH2 bending and symmetrical CH3 deformation, respectively [30]. Further, the out-of-plane CH bending of monosaccharide rings corresponds to the peak at 895 cm−1.

Regarding the spectrum of ɣ-alumina (Fig. 4b), the band at 3456 cm−1 corresponds to the OH stretching vibration of groups attached to the Al3+ ions, and the CH stretching is found at 2900 cm−1, showing the presence of a small amount of urea, which is consistent with the observations reported earlier [21]. The band at 1774 cm−1 is attributed to the H-O-H bending, indicating the presence of hydration water, and that at 1136 cm−1 to the Al-O-H bending mode [45]. Further, the AlO stretching vibration appears at 812 cm−1 [20], and the peaks in the range of 700–960 cm−1 correspond to the tetrahedral structure of Al3+ in gamma form.

The spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 4.c) shows a band centered at 3455 cm−1 assigned to OH and NH stretching vibrations. The peak at 1384 cm−1 is attributed to the OH bending, and the bands at 1637 cm−1, 1493 cm−1 and 1025 cm−1 correspond to the vibrations of C-O-C bonds, CH2 and CN, respectively. These bands confirm the presence of hydroxyl, amine and CO functional groups, respectively, in agreement with the results reported earlier [26,46]. Further, a weak peak was identified at 1725 cm−1, ascribed to the carboxylic acids on the CQDs surface. All these facts corroborate the successful synthesis of the CQDs.

The spectrum of CS/γAl2O3 spectrum is displayed in Fig. 4d. Compared to neat CS and Al, the peak associated with the OH stretching is shifted towards higher wavenumbers and appears at about 3566 cm−1. The peak ascribed to aliphatic CH stretching vibrations is shifted to 2923 cm−1. In addition, the CO stretching of the primary amide changes from 1589 cm−1 to 1599 cm−1, and the peak related to CN stretching vibrations of ternary amide bonds is shifted down to 1320 cm−1. All these shifts are indicative of the intermolecular interactions between CS and ɣ-alumina [20,21]. The presence of all the characteristic peaks of CS in the spectrum of the mixture with only some changes in the wavenumber indicates that the incorporation of γ-Al into this biopolymer does not modify its structure. Similarly, the bands of gamma alumina in the mixture are slightly shifted (ie. the AlO stretching vibration shifts from 810 to 812 cm−1). This supports that γAl2O3 nanoparticles are present in the mixture with hardly modification.

The spectrum of the CS/ γAL2O3/CQDs is shown in Fig. 4e. All the characteristic peaks of the constituents can be observed in the spectrum of the mixture, with very small shifts in frequency or intensity, indicating that the presence of CQDs in the CS/ γAL2O3 nanocarrier did not yield any new peak or materially alter it [47,48].

The characteristic vibrations of all the nanocomposite components can be observed in the spectrum of CS/γAl2O3/CQDs/CUR nanocarrier (Fig. 4f). The presence of a peak at 1074 cm−1 corresponding to the C-O-CH3 group of CUR supports the successful incorporation of the drug in the nanocomposite [49]. Besides, the peak at 1642 cm−1 is wider and stronger compared to CS/γAl2O3/CQDs, ascribed to the additional CO groups of CUR, which appear in the free drug at 1628 cm−1 [16]. Furthermore, the broadening of the region corresponding to the OH stretching at about 3440 cm−1 suggests increased hydrogen bond formation in the mixture upon incorporation of CUR. In fact, the hydroxyl groups of CUR and the hydroxyl and amino groups of CS as well as the OH groups of ɣ-alumina and the hydroxyl, amino and carboxylic acid groups of CQDs can interact via hydrogen bonding. Also, the CO and NH stretching bands in CUR-loaded CS/γAl2O3/CQD nanocarrier are clearly weaker and are shifted to lower wavenumbers compared to the ternary nanocomposite, another indication of the interactions between the drug and the nanocomposite via hydrogen bonding and polar interactions. On the whole, the comparison of the FTIR spectra proves that all the constituents were present in the nanocomposite hydrogel.

XRD analysis was carried out to get information about the crystalline nature of the samples, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 . Regarding the XRD pattern of ɣ-alumina (Fig. 5a), the peaks at 2θ values of 33°, 45.9°, and 67° correspond to the reflections of the 311, 400, and 440 crystalline planes [20], respectively, corroborating its high level of crystallinity. The average crystallite size (Dp) of the nanoparticles can be calculated using Scherrer Equation [50]:
(6)
D p
=
Kλ
β cos θ
where θ is the Bragg angle, β the full width at half maximum of peak, λ the X-ray wavelength and k a shape factor usually taken as 0.89. For the characteristic peaks at 33°, 45.9°, and 67°, Dp was calculated to be 15.4, 13.6 and 9.4 respectively. This indicates that the synthetized alumina nanocrystals have an average size of 12.8 nm.

The XRD pattern of CS (Fig. 5b) shows a broad characteristic peak at 2θ of 20.2°, corroborating the semi-crystalline nature of the polymer [16]. The pattern of the CQDs in Fig. 5c reveals a broad peak at approximately 2θ = 25.5° that is related to the (002) plane of graphite-like structures [22], corresponding to an interlayer spacing of 0.348 nm. This wide peak indicates the amorphous phase of carbon, in agreement with results reported previously [26,46,51]. The disordered structure is ascribed to the introduction of oxygen-containing functional groups [51].

The diffractogram of CS/ɣ-alumina in Fig. 5d reveals a wide peak centered at approximately 22°, significantly broader than that of neat CS, indicative of a reduction in the level of crystallinity of the biopolymer in the mixture due to the addition of the inorganic nanoparticles. Upon addition of the CQDs (Fig. 5e), the peak intensity was reduced, indicative of a further drop in the level of crystallinity, due to the amorphous nature of the CQDs. The changes in peak intensity also point out the interaction between the nanocomposite components [21]. The effect of adding CUR to the nanosystem is depicted in Fig. 5f. A close analysis of the diffractogram shows peaks at 2θ of 17.6, 23.18, and 43.74°, which are related to the crystalline nature of this anticancer drug. These results demonstrate the successful loading of the drug in the nanocomposite [52].

The poor solubility of CUR, which results in a low bioavailability, is one of the main obstacles to its use. To address this issue, hydrogel nanocarriers containing CS, alumina, and CQDS were used in this study to trap CUR drug through the interconnected structure of the crosslinked network, with the aim to increase the percentage of drug loading and entrapment [27]. More drug molecules can be captured in the nanocarrier via incorporating nanoparticles with a porous structure. The drug loading and entrapment efficiency were calculated to be 48.25 % and 86.25 %, respectively. The loading and encapsulation percentages were higher than those reported previously for other CUR-loaded nanocarriers [16,53–55]. Thus, 14 % loading was calculated for CS/alginate [53], 37 % loading was reported for CS/PAA [16], 12 % entrapment efficiency was reported for alginate-CS-pluronic nanocomposite [54] and 82 % for CS-DEX-PEG nanocarrier [55]. The high specific surface area of γ-alumina, which is rich in hydroxyl groups that are prone to interact with CUR via polar and hydrogen bonding interactions, as corroborated by the FTIR spectra, could account for the improved drug loading and entrapment efficiencies attained in this work. Further, a high load percentage of the drug can be anchored to the porous structure of γ-alumina, which can trap more molecules inside. The values obtained herein demonstrate that the developed nanocarrier is appropriate for drug delivery applications.

Among factors that can trigger drug release are light, pH, temperature, sound waves, magnetic field, etc. One of the most suitable stimuli for cancer therapy is pH, since the pH of the tumor microenvironment is noticeably lower than that of healthy tissues; therefore, using pH-triggered nanocarriers is a smart approach for enhancing the therapeutic effects of antitumor drugs. The in vitro release of CUR was investigated using the dialysis technique at 37 °C in two different media: healthy tissue (pH = 7.4) and cancerous tissue (pH = 5.4), and the results are shown in Fig. 6 . An initial burst release is observed for the first 3 h until the release percentage reaches 20 %, followed by a gradual and more sustained release. However, after 12 h, the percentage of drug released in an acid environment is considerably higher than in a neutral environment, in agreement with previous studies [7,16,18–20]. Thus, within the first 24 h, 38 % and 63 % of CUR was released in neutral and acidic media, respectively. After 72 h, 68 % and 90 % of CUR was discharged in healthy and cancerous tissues, respectively, and the difference was even more pronounced for 96 h (72 % and 99 %). These results demonstrate the strong pH sensitivity of the developed nanocarrier, leading to a more efficient drug release in an acidic medium.

The emulsion process was used in this work to shape the nanocarrier and increase its stability. A CS-based hydrogel was modified with ɣ-Al2O3 and CQDs nanoparticles and then loaded with CUR, and the drug was released from the ensemble in a pH-sensitive manner, due to the pH sensitivity of the three components. It is well known than CS forms pH-sensitive hydrogels [13], since it has a pKa of 6.0. At acidic pH (<6), the amino groups of CS are positively charged but at higher pH the polymer deprotonates and loses the charge. Regarding ɣAl2O3, at pH lower than 6, the surface gets positively charged through protonation of the Al3O groups, while at basic pH the surface becomes negatively charged through deprotonation of the AlOH2 groups [56]. Besides, CQDs have been reported to show pH-sensitive behavior, and the sensitivity depends on the synthesis method and the reaction conditions [57]. Overall, results reveal a complete CUR release at tumor pH, stating an increased circulation time and improved controlled release pattern. The release is more significant in a cancerous environment than in healthy tissues, indicating fewer toxic effects of CUR on non-cancerous cells. Thus, the drug is properly and slowly released at neutral pH, which reduces the side effects on the human body and leads to lower drug consumption [27–30].

Experimental drug release data were analyzed using a variety of kinetic models to better understand the mechanisms and kinetics of drug release at both neutral and acid pH [32–36], and the results are collected in Table 3 . The goodness of fit, which can be deduced from the value of R2, was used to identify the best-fitting model. R2 values closer to 1 imply a better degree of fit. For both pH studied, experimental data were properly fitted with the Korsmeyer-Peppas, Higuchi, Hixson-Crowell, Weibull, Peppas-Salhin, and Hopfenberg models, showing R2 values higher than 0.96, indicative of a very good agreement between the experimental and predicted values. At pH 7.4, the Higuchi and Hopfenberg models have the highest correlation coefficient (R2 value of 0.99). The former model addresses the rate of drug release from a polymer matrix in which the solute concentration exceeds its solubility [32], while the second describes drug release from polymers that could be degraded or eroded during drug loss [36]. On the other hand, at pH = 5.4, the Korsmeyer-Peppas model provides the best fitting; this model is used when the mechanism of drug release is complex and involves multiple mechanisms [33].

The constants of the equations for the different models, such as n, k, β or m were also calculated (Table 3). The diffusional exponent (n) gives information about the drug release mechanism. Thus, n ≤ 0.5 indicates that the drug release follows a Fickian diffusion, while a value of n in the range of 0.5 < n < 1, indicates that the drug release mechanism is anomalous transport, and that it is controlled by non-Fickian diffusion [36]. Therefore, according to the data shown in Table 3, the drug release follows a Fickian diffusion mechanism for both pH studied, in agreement with the results reported for other CS-based nanocarriers for CUR delivery [16,21].

The cytotoxic activity of the synthesized nanocomposites and the growth inhibition impacts of free CUR, CS, CS/ɣ-alumina, CS/ɣ-alumina/GQDs and CS/ɣ-alumina/GQD/CUR on breast tumor cell line (MCF-7) were investigated via MTT assay carried out after a 72-h incubation period using a group of untreated cells as a control group. Tests were performed in triplicate, and the mean value of the experiments is reported (Fig. 7 ). Both the percentage of live cells (cell viability) and the percentage of cell inhibition (in vitro cell cytotoxicity) are shown. The results indicate that CS/γAl2O3/CQDs/CUR nanocarrier, which results in a cell inhibition of 48 % (cell viability of 52 %), is responsible for the highest cell death. According to these findings, this nanocarrier has great potential for drug delivery and can accelerate CUR penetration into breast cancer cells. Experimental data demonstrate that the developed nanocarrier has adequate cytotoxicity compared to free CUR. Similar to other chemotherapeutic agents, CUR displays some adverse effects during treatment, including nausea, diarrhea, headache, and skin rash. Due to the pH-responsiveness of the synthesized nanocarrier, it can be used for the targeted delivery of CUR in order to avoid its side effects. Therefore, less drug is required to be injected and side effects are minimized. Overall, experimental results indicate the good biocompatibility and suitability of the developed nanocomposite to enhance the anti-proliferative effect of cancer cells and thereby suppress the growth and proliferation of tumor cells.

The effect of the nanocarrier and the different samples on the start of cell necrosis and apoptosis was examined via flow cytometry test, and the results are shown in Fig. 8 . For comparative purposes, a control sample of the same cells was also cultured under the same conditions. In this sample, 81.8 % of the cells were in the live cell phase, 15.6 % were in the necrosis phase, 0.81 % were in the early apoptosis phase and 1.81 % in the late apoptosis phase [16]. Free CUR has a higher percentage of necrotic cells (24.3 %) than the nanocarrier (9.22 %), likely due to the use of the double emulsion method. Further, the amount of necrotic cells in free CUR is significantly higher than in the control sample (about 56 % increment). Additionally, CUR shows more cells in the secondary phase of apoptosis. On the other hand, the drug-free nanocarrier decreased the percentage of live cells to 61.1 % and the number of necrotic cells to 9.22 % compared to the control sample.

Regarding the cells in the secondary phase, the percentage increased from 0.81 % in the control sample to 66.6 % in the nanocarrier hydrogel containing CUR drug. It has also drastically reduced the number of viable cells, in agreement with former studies [16,21]. According to the results of the cytotoxicity test, the concentration of free CUR in living cells (21.9 %) is higher than in the drug-loaded nanocarrier (66.6 %). This finding suggests that CUR increases cytotoxicity via enhancement of apoptosis concurrently with drug release. The result is a slow release over time and programmed cell death due to an interaction between the drug and the nanocarrier containing curcumin.

In this research, a hydrogel nanocarrier comprising CS, γ-Al2O3 and CQDs was prepared via double nanoemulsion method for the targeted release of CUR as a model drug in breast cancer tissue. XRD confirmed the successful synthesis of the nanocomposite and revealed its crystalline nature. The results of FTIR analysis corroborated the strong interaction between CUR and the nanocomposite hydrogel. According to FESEM images, the nanocarrier had a quasi-spherical shape, which is the most suitable for use in DDS, with an average hydrodynamic diameter of 227 nm, as measured by DLS, and a zeta potential of −37.8 mV, indicative of a high colloidal stability. High drug loading and entrapment efficiencies were attained (86 and 48 %, respectively). A sustained and pH-sensitive drug release from the nanocarriers was observed for 96 h, with 99 % and 72 % of CUR released at pH 5.4 and 7.4, respectively, indicating that the designed nanocarrier is more sensitive to the tumor environment than the pH of healthy tissues. The best models to describe the drug release behavior in terms of the correlation coefficient were the Higuchi and Hopfenberg for healthy tissues and the Korsmeyer-Peppas for cancerous tissues. According to MTT test, the nanocarrier resulted in approximately 40 % cell death. Further, flow cytometry analysis indicated that it induced about 67 % of MCF-7 breast cancer cells to undergo apoptosis. It also drastically reduced the number of viable cells. Nonetheless, additional in vivo animal experiments should be performed to assess the internal drug toxicity of CS/γAl2O3/CQDs prior to going through the clinical trial stages. Further, to become a commercially available product, in vivo studies on humans are required. Progress may also be made by reducing the drug side effects and residues in the body and increasing its shelf life. Overall, the proposed nanocarrier has valuable properties as a regulatory, targeted and pH-responsive drug delivery system for the sustained release of curcumin as an antitumor agent.
